Q1i.scenario: Healthy Life Insurance (HLI) is a well-established insurer that has been writing Critical Illness (CI) insurance for many years. HLI has recently noticed an increase in the number of CI claims being declined.
Q1i.question: Describe the possible reasons behind the increase in the numbers of repudiated/declined claims. [Max 10]

Increase in the number of claims: The increase in the number of claims being declined could simply be due to an increase in the number of claims being made — this could just be a result of increasing volumes of CI business on the insurer's books, e.g. in response to successful marketing initiatives.

Alternatively, increased numbers of claims could be due to: a maturing book of business where the effect of initial underwriting wears off as policies increase in duration; an adverse change in mix of business (e.g. an increase in the average age of policyholders); a general increasing trend in critical illness incidence, e.g. as a result of medical advances or early diagnosis of diseases.
However, as well as the absolute number of claims, the proportion of claims that are declined should also be considered.
Improved claims control: An increase in the proportion of claims being declined could be as a result of improvements in the claim control process (e.g. using more experienced staff), leading to a reduction in the number (or proportion) of invalid claims being paid.
Claims that are not valid: More policyholders may be trying to claim for conditions that they are not entitled to because they do not understand exactly what the policy is covering — because the precise cover was not explained properly when sold; the policyholder was misled; the policy wording is not clear enough; the policy conditions might have changed; or the policyholder has simply forgotten what the policy covers.
A new disease may have emerged that is not covered by the policy but because the policyholder considers it critical they may try to claim.

There may be cases where the policyholder understands what is covered but tries to claim anyway — more likely if the insurer has a reputation for paying claims that do not quite meet the claim criteria, or if the policyholder has been encouraged by an unscrupulous broker.
Some policyholders may even try to fraudulently claim for a condition they do not have. An increase in such claims may be more likely if economic conditions have deteriorated recently.
Not eligible / misrepresentation: There may have been an increase in policyholders who think they are entitled to cover, but it turns out during the claim checking process that they have breached a condition of cover — most commonly where non-disclosure of medical evidence at purchase would have affected the cover provided or premium charged.

Non-disclosure may be the result of: poor selling practices (seller not explaining the importance of disclosing all material facts); insurer not clearly asking for all relevant information on application forms; or deliberate misrepresentation by the policyholder.
There may be other changes that arose during the policy term, such as not keeping up to date with premium payments, that has made the cover invalid.

Q1ii.scenario: Same as Q1(i). HLI has noticed an increase in the number of CI claims being declined.
Q1ii.question: Outline the implications for the insurer of an increase in declined claims. [Max 7]

Implications for the insurer: To the extent that the increase in declined claims is due to improvements in claims control, this (at first sight) is actually good news — it means reduced claims costs and it is more likely that cover will be as intended when premiums were calculated. Also, this will help the insurer to avoid getting a reputation for paying out claims unnecessarily.

However, if a significant number of claims are being declined, many of those claimants will be expecting a claim payout and so will be disappointed. Dealing with the complaints from these customers may cause additional administration and associated expenses, and should be dealt with carefully using well-trained and sympathetic staff.

There is a danger of building up a reputation for selling policies that do not pay out, so in the long run people will be less willing to take out the insurer's CI products, thinking that the cover is worthless. This will lead to lower sales volumes.
Brokers will also be upset if they have to be involved with disputed claims and deal with unhappy customers — and so may be less willing to sell the insurer's products.
Some disputed claims may subsequently be settled in favour of the customers (e.g. by the financial ombudsman), leading to more claims being paid than the insurer originally intended.
If the increase in the number of declined claims is due to worsening claim incidence, then this has implications for the insurer's profitability and solvency.

The increase in declined claims may be an indication that policies are not being sold appropriately — hence, there may be higher than expected lapse rates and there may be further claim disputes in the future.

Q1iii.scenario: Same as Q1(i). HLI's Board has asked for recommended analysis and actions to address the increase in declined claims.
Q1iii.question: Outline the recommended analysis and actions that HLI should undertake. [Max 15]

Claims experience analysis: Carry out an analysis of the claims experience and exposure over the period of investigation to assess to what extent the increase in the declined claim figures has simply been due to an increase in the number of claims.
The factors contributing to an increase in claim numbers should be identified and quantified to assess how they compare with the assumptions in the company's models for pricing or financial planning.

If the increase in declined claims has been in line with these assumptions, then this does not suggest that the increase is a cause of concern in itself (otherwise, further investigation needs to be made).

Each declined claim should be investigated to assess the reason for the claim being declined — at a high level (e.g. claim conditions not met or misrepresentation) the reason should be readily identifiable from claim records. However, identifying more detail (e.g. poor selling processes) may require subjective judgement.

Investigate whether claims control has improved over the period and what impact this is expected to have had — e.g. a recent sample of declined claims could be assessed as to whether they would have still been declined under previous claims control measures.
Analysis split by factors: The proportion of claims declined could be analysed, split by: age of policyholder; duration of policy (a high level of claims being declined shortly after contract inception suggests misrepresentation or possibly fraud); distribution channel and down to individual seller (to identify if there is a problem with particular sellers); type of claim (to help assess whether particular claim definitions are causing problems).

A key problem with any detailed analysis will be lack of claim volumes, and so there may be too few declined claims to be able to make sound conclusions. However, they will give some indication of where attention should initially be focused.
Sales and underwriting actions: The sales process should be reviewed, particularly for those channels or sellers where a potential problem has been identified. Action could include re-training, regular monitoring and introducing remuneration that is (partly) geared to rewarding low proportions of claims being declined.

Consider reviewing application forms, marketing literature and policy wording to see how these can be improved — ensuring that all necessary information is captured, making clear the importance of disclosing all relevant information, clarifying that only the listed conditions are covered and that these are clearly defined.
The insurer could ensure that the penalties for misrepresentation are made very clear to the customer when the policy is sold.

It could consider strengthening the initial underwriting (e.g. collecting and using more medical evidence) to pick up more cases of misrepresentation — however, this will incur higher costs and may inconvenience sellers and potential customers.

The policy design could be reviewed, e.g. removing benefits that are causing a great number of claims to be declined. Alternatively, the insurer could try to target customers less likely to make invalid claims.
It should try to make regular communication with existing customers, reminding them of these issues and educating them as to what are valid claims.
Industry and pricing implications: If a new critical disease has emerged that is not currently covered, the insurer may decide to include this in new policies (and existing ones if reviewable).

Investigate whether competitors are experiencing similar rates of declined claims — this may help identify whether there is a problem with the insurer's particular product or processes, or whether this is an industry-wide problem. If industry-wide, the insurance industry might pool resources to improve things (e.g. improving claim definitions or sharing data).
If figures reflect more stringent claims control, the insurer might consider reducing premium rates or relaxing claim validation criteria.
The insurer could, as some insurers in overseas markets, publish declined claim figures to highlight to sellers the consequences of not selling appropriate cover.
Depending on the extent to which these recommended measures are successful, the insurer will need to allow for the implications of declined claims in future pricing, reserving and financial planning.

Moving forward, a system of monitoring declined claims should be put into place, including keeping a record of the reason for the claim and for declining it. This will enable the impact of actions to be regularly monitored.
The insurer should also monitor the knock-on effects of high levels of claims declined, such as poor persistency and new business volumes, and take steps to improve these.

Q1iv.scenario: Same as Q1(i). HLI's risk management team has asked for a formula for calculating the impact of changes in the Available Solvency Margin (ASM) and Required Solvency Margin (RSM) on the Solvency Ratio (SR).
Q1iv.question: Derive the formula to calculate the change in Solvency Ratio for small changes in ASM and RSM. [3]

Solvency ratio change formula: = Change in ASM as % of RSM − SR × change in RSM as percentage of RSM

Q1v.scenario: HLI's solvency position has changed during the current month due to several events. Opening position: ASM = 1231 cr, RSM = 403 cr. Events: (a) net increase in RSM for individual business of 8 cr; (b) decrease in RSM for group business by 3 cr; (c) loss of 27 cr from demographic sources; (d) profit of 3 cr from economic sources; (e) capital injection of 50 cr by shareholders.
Q1v.question: Calculate the current month solvency ratio and the impact of each event on the solvency ratio using the formula derived in Q1(iv). [5]

Solvency ratio calculation: (a) Net increase in RSM for individual business of 8 cr: RSM becomes 411 → SR = 1231/411 = 299.51%. Impact = −5.95%.
(b) Decrease in RSM for group business by 3 cr: RSM becomes 408 → SR = 1231/408 = 301.72%. Impact = +2.20%.
(c) Loss of 27 cr from demographic sources: ASM becomes 1204 → SR = 1204/408 = 295.10%. Impact = −6.62%.
(d) Profit of 3 cr from economic sources: ASM becomes 1207 → SR = 1207/408 = 295.83%. Impact = +0.74%.
(e) Capital injection of 50 cr by shareholders: ASM becomes 1257 → SR = 1257/408 = 308.09%. Impact = +12.25%.

Q1vi.scenario: Same as Q1(iv). HLI also has ULIP (unit-linked insurance plan) business in its policyholder fund. The Board has asked about the appropriate investment strategy for the shareholder's fund.
Q1vi.question: Discuss the appropriate investment strategy for the shareholder's fund supporting the ASM, including the impact of ULIP equity funds. [5]

Investment strategy for shareholder's fu: The assets in the shareholder's fund contributing to ASM have two components: (1) assets supporting the minimum regulatory level of solvency; (2) the fund in excess of the regulatory minimum.

Since the RSM represents a proxy of the risk of the insurer's liability book, it is expected to move similarly to the movement of policy liabilities. Accordingly, the portion of the shareholder's fund supporting the regulatory minimum level of solvency should have investments in various asset classes in the same proportion as in the policyholder's fund.

When the solvency level is marginally above the regulatory minimum, cash is probably the best preferred asset category for the excess assets because: there are no problems with marketability or asset values; it is secure in monetary terms; there is a link between short-term interest rates and inflation providing some limited inflation protection; small size investment is not a problem and its value doesn't fluctuate much with market movement.

However, when the SR is significantly higher than the regulatory minimum, the excess fund (over regulatory minimum) may be invested to achieve higher expected returns — a suitable proportion of investments in equity, bonds and property may be appropriate, while the fund up to the regulatory minimum retains the same investment as before.

Since in ULIP, the unit fund and reserve move with market values of assets and the RSM also moves similarly (as it is related to fund value), a corresponding proportion of equity investment is made in ASM to ensure consistent movements in RSM and ASM for equity market movements. This is crucial where significant ULIP equity fund investments are present — otherwise, for an upward equity market movement, the RSM will increase without any supporting increase in ASM, causing downward pressure on solvency.

Q1vii.scenario: HLI is a medium-size Health & Care Insurance company in India with major market share in Individual & Group Critical Illness products. It also has significant unit-linked (ULIP) business. The CEO has asked for your opinion on equity investments in the shareholder's fund given the presence of ULIP business.
Q1vii.question: Explain the rationale for equity investments in the shareholder's fund due to the presence of Unit Linked business, and provide your response. [5]

Equity investments in shareholder fund —: Since in ULIP, the unit fund and reserve move with the market values of the assets, the RSM also moves similarly as it is related to the fund value. [½]
To have a similar movement in the fund supporting ASM in line with the market movement of the ULIP equity funds, the corresponding proportion of equity investment is made in ASM. [1]
This will ensure consistent movements in RSM and ASM for equity market movements. [1]

This is crucial where significant ULIP equity fund investments are present in the policyholder's fund — otherwise, for an upward equity market movement, the RSM will increase without any supporting increase in ASM, causing downward pressure on solvency. [1]
So far, the company doesn't have any equity investment in its shareholder's fund — this should be rectified by introducing equity in proportion to the ULIP equity fund exposure. [½]

Q2i.scenario: HealthX is a medium-sized standalone health insurance company writing Group Medical Reimbursement (GMR) for inpatient (IP) cover. HealthX is considering extending its product portfolio to cover outpatient (OP) expenses as well. It is currently evaluating a combined IP+OP product.
Q2i.question: Outline the important features of a product covering IP benefits only. [½ per point; Max 3]

IP-only product features: Reimburses medical expenses incurred in connection with hospitalisation due to sickness or injury. Requires minimum hours/days of hospitalisation — typically 24/48 hours.
Yearly reviewable — premium rates/terms could vary year on year based on the experience of the insured pool. Annual Maximum Limit (AML) — maximum claim amount payable in a year.
Sub-limits on hospital room charges, pharmacy etc. depending upon the network chosen. Cashless settlement for treatment within the network of providers; reimbursement for outside network.
Co-pay/deductibles — claims up to a certain limit to be borne by the insured. Cosmetic and other treatments without medical necessity excluded. Optional coverage for maternity, dental and optical.

Q2ii.scenario: Same as Q2(i). HealthX is designing the OP cover component of the combined IP+OP product.
Q2ii.question: Suggest the design features for the OP cover.

OP cover design features: No experience on OP — introduce minimal benefits to start with, with significant co-pay/deductible and a yearly reviewable structure with AML.
Cover consultation, labs and pharmacy costs; exclude cosmetic treatments. Consider allowing alternative treatments (homeopathy, ayurvedic etc.) to increase the product appeal.
Allow specialist consultation only upon GP referral to minimise cost and avoid abuse.

Benchmark with what is already offered in the market or elsewhere — do not want to offer benefits richer than the market to avoid anti-selection. Offer different network options with differentiated pricing — this allows pricing segmentation and choices for customers based on affordability.
Clearly define what is OP to avoid overlap with IP (e.g. day surgery). For individuals and voluntary coverage under groups, some level of underwriting will be required.

Consider offering health check-up packages to enhance the product appeal and help prevent some lifestyle diseases. Build-in disease management programmes to effectively manage lifestyle diseases such as diabetes and hypertension.
Consider no/low claim bonus in the form of reduction in renewal premium — could be a differentiator and disincentivise small claims.

Q2iii.scenario: Same as Q2(i-ii). HealthX is now pricing the OP cover component.
Q2iii.question: Describe the steps involved in pricing the OP cover.

Pricing steps for OP cover: Burning cost = frequency × severity. Frequency refers to the incidence of claims; severity refers to the size of claims.
Estimate the frequency and severity by each of the major risk factors for a typical standard life: age, gender, network, occupation, location.
Dental/optical and maternity coverage costs need to be considered separately.
Leverage any industry studies on burning cost or take the reinsurer's help. Align the burning cost to the product design — such as co-pay/deductibles and other limits.

Underwriting loading to reflect additional risk for sub-standard lives — work with the underwriting and claims function to estimate the loading for different types of sub-standard risks. The existing IP underwriting framework could be the starting point but needs adjustment as even a moderate sub-standard could lead to higher frequency of OP compared to IP claims.
Estimate operational expenses based on projected volumes — be cognisant of higher frequency of OP claims and likely much wider network of providers to manage.
There are initial product development and system setup costs that would need to be recouped over time — an allowance for this will need to go into the pricing.
Build loadings on top of burning cost to reflect the expected return on capital and profitability targets — simply, it could be a target loss ratio (claims/premium) that meets the requirements.
Competitive considerations will drive how long we need to spread fixed costs over and the loading for profitability.
Profit testing and market testing will be iterative until a good balance is struck.

Q2iv.scenario: Same as Q2(i-iii). HealthX needs to prepare its underwriting, claims, valuation and reinsurance functions for the OP cover.
Q2iv.question: Describe the considerations and operational changes required in each of the following areas for the OP cover: (a) Underwriting; (b) Claims; (c) Valuation; (d) Reinsurance. [Max 5 each = 20]

a) Underwriting: The insurer will be primarily concerned about high frequency and low severity business. From an underwriting perspective, key concerns are Pre-Existing and Chronic cases — items such as diabetes, hypertension, upper respiratory tract disease, and disease of the circulatory system.

Mechanisms to protect against anti-selection: medical declaration upfront including tests and diagnosis; simplified underwriting declaration; moratorium underwriting (PEC not covered for a certain period); waiting period (say 6 months before pre-existing or chronic claims are covered). Regulatory guidance on minimum product requirements and conditions on pre-existing/chronic cases will be relevant.

Product design recommendations: imposing co-payment, deductible or co-sharing; consultation subject to a fixed deductible; pharmacy subject to co-payments or switch to generics; caps or co-insurances or pre-authorisation for items prone to abuse such as physiotherapy; sub-limits as an important cost control.

Credibility approach may require change because there will be far more claims of less severity. The underwriting team will also need to be cognisant of fraud, waste and abuse in the market. Underwriters will need training on the product features and tweaks to existing underwriting practices.
b) Claims: Claims will need to be prepared for a major increase in claims frequency and major reduction in claims severity. Need to decide on the pre-authorisation level — if set low, a higher proportion of claims will be subject to scrutiny, which may impair service delivery.

Clear interaction between claims and underwriting teams is needed: the claims team must be mindful of the underwriting approach taken; the exclusions specified; the product specifics (limits/sub-limits/co-pay/deductible); and the level of claims reporting by sub-categories/sub-plans and by networks.

The claims admin system (in-house or TPA) must accommodate OP claims: pre-authorisation request and response TAT; high frequency/low severity claims processing; linkage to CPT/ICD-9/10 classification; whether the regulator enables fee-for-service or bundled payments; pharmacy prescription pre-authorisation; pharmacy benefit management systems; fraud, waste and abuse flagging; claims reporting and analytics; a mobile app for claims submission.
The insurer must decide how to treat claims from outside the preferred network or from abroad.
c) Valuation: OP may have different claim pattern/seasonality — e.g. some services such as annual health screening or dental/optical could mostly occur during holiday season or towards end of the policy year. Valuation needs to be cognisant of this when setting up unexpired risk reserves.

Claim reporting lag will vary — most OP claims do not typically require pre-authorisation. This will depend upon how claims are split between cashless and reimbursement and how digitalised the claims data flow is. Valuation must consider this when setting up IBNR.

On IBNER, there will be differences between OP and IP — IP will have estimates initially at pre-authorisation and the actuals might significantly differ once final bills are negotiated, whereas OP tends not to vary much.
Valuation systems may require changes to handle the large volume of claims data with significantly higher volume of smaller claims.
d) Reinsurance: Having OP product will dampen the requirement for catastrophe claims reinsurance as the claims are much higher in frequency and less severe in severity. The insurer may need to be mindful of the possibility of epidemic/pandemic which may further increase frequency of claims.

An excess of loss may not be entirely relevant as the emphasis is probably more on: capacity requirement; underwriting support; claims support; supportive actuarial pricing and net risk rates; solvency requirement and solvency relief; technical support on disease management, chronic disease management, preventative care, diagnostics, digital health and wellness.

From that angle, it may make more sense to engage in a traditional quota-share arrangement where a certain proportion is ceded to the reinsurer (both premiums and claims) — this may protect the insurer against the risk of having underpriced while still getting appropriate solvency relief.
A cost (reinsurance outgo) vs benefit (capital efficiency; reduced volatility) analysis is required to put the appropriate reinsurance structure in place.

Q2v.scenario: Same as Q2(i). The management of HealthX is debating whether it should offer an IP-only product or combined IP+OP. The CFO argues that OP insurance is less necessary than IP insurance.
Q2v.question: Comment on the merits of the CFO's argument.

CFO's argument — merits and counterpoint: OP tends to be more frequent and less severe. IP is on the other hand less frequent and more severe. While absence of insurance for IP could hurt individuals significantly, it could be argued that the insurance necessity is lower for OP as the severity tends to be low.

However, OP timing and frequency could pose significant financial burden (e.g. several members of a family falling sick at the same time) — so, there is still OP cover need for some, especially low/middle income earners.
OP is arguably more uncertain and high risk for HealthX — more prone to fraud, waste and abuse.
On the other hand, low premium volume if it is IP only — so, fixed expenses will have to be spread over a smaller volume.
Absence of OP insurance would also mean less flow of payout to providers through insurance, and hence reduced negotiation power for lower tariffs by HealthX.

Q2vi.scenario: Same as Q2(i). A consultant has suggested that HealthX should consider capitation as the claims settlement method for the OP cover, under which the provider receives a fixed payment per person per year for providing OP services.
Q2vi.question: Comment on the merits and challenges of the capitation suggestion.

Merits of capitation: Under capitation, the provider receives a fixed payment per person per year for providing OP services required under the insurance policy, in lieu of fee-for-service. Essentially, the utilisation risk is passed on to the providers.
OP is more prone to waste, abuse and fraud — this arrangement emphasises efficiency of care as provider profits decrease with higher utilisation.
So, there is merit in the suggestion given that OP is a new risk to HealthX.
Challenges of capitation: It is typically hard to agree on blanket capitation with providers. It is easier to estimate the cost for existing business as opposed to new business — thus making it even more difficult for the providers to agree on the capitation fee.
Insured members may not be happy if the providers compromise on the quality of care. Providers could convert some OP into IP.
An insured member of HealthX may have to go to different providers for OP and IP, which could be considered inconvenient.
Regulations may not permit such an arrangement.
